title: Worksheet 6: Lists & Indexing
description: Learn to create lists, access items by index, and loop through sequences in Python.

problems.title: Lists — you already know them!
problems.content:
Remember writing for i in [1, 2, 3]: in Worksheet 3? That [1, 2, 3] is a list!

A list holds multiple items in order, wrapped in square brackets [] and separated by commas:

numbers = [10, 20, 30]
words = ['hello', 'world']
mixed = [1, 'two', 3]

You can store a list in a variable and use it later — just like you do with numbers and strings.
problems.footer: A list is items inside square brackets [], separated by commas.

problems.title: You've seen this before!
problems.content: Remember this from Worksheet 3? You used lists inside for loops to repeat code with different values.
problems.task: Run this code — it's the same kind of loop you wrote in Worksheet 3.
problems.hint: Just press the 'Run Code' button to see the output.

problems.title: Storing a list in a variable
problems.content: That ['red', 'blue', 'green'] is a list. Just like numbers and text, you can store a list in a variable!
problems.task: Run this code to see what happens when you print a whole list.
problems.hint: The whole list gets printed, square brackets and all!

problems.title: Make a bigger list
problems.content: Lists can be any length! This one has 3 numbers, but let's make it bigger.
problems.task: Change the list so it contains five numbers: 10, 20, 30, 40, 50.
problems.hint: Replace everything inside the square brackets: [10, 20, 30, 40, 50].

problems.title: Don't forget the brackets!
problems.content: A list in Python must be wrapped in square brackets []. If you forget them, Python treats the items differently!
problems.task: Run the code. Notice the output has () parentheses instead of [] square brackets — that means it's not a proper list! Add square brackets around the items to fix it.
problems.hint: Wrap the items in square brackets: fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

rules.message: Your list needs square brackets []

problems.title: Picking items: how indexing works
problems.content:
Every item in a list has a position number called an index. Python starts counting from 0, not 1!

Index012
Item'cat''dog''fish'

To get a single item, write the list name followed by the index in square brackets: animals[0] gives 'cat'.
problems.footer: Python starts counting from 0, not 1. The first item is always at index 0.

problems.title: Your first index
problems.content: Let's try it! Use an index to pick out a single item from a list.
problems.task: Run this code to see the first animal in the list.
problems.hint: [0] means "the first item." Python starts counting from 0.

problems.title: Pick a different animal
problems.content: Change the index number to pick a different animal from the list.
problems.task: Change [0] to [2] to print the third animal.
problems.hint: The third item is at index 2 (remember: 0, 1, 2).

rules.message: Use animals[2] to get the third item

problems.title: The off-by-one trap!
problems.content: This is a very common mistake! If a list has 3 items, the valid indices are 0, 1, and 2 — not 1, 2, and 3.
problems.task: Run this code to see the error. The list has 3 items but the code tries index 3. Fix it so it prints the last item.
problems.hint: A 3-item list has indices 0, 1, and 2. The last item is at index 2, not 3.

rules.message: Use pets[2] to get the last item in a 3-item list

problems.title: Print two items
problems.content: You can use indexing multiple times to print different items from the same list. Lists of numbers work the same way as lists of words!
problems.task: Write two print() statements to print the first and last scores from the list.
problems.hint: Use print(scores[0]) for the first score and print(scores[3]) for the last.

rules.message: Use scores[0] to get the first score
Use scores[3] to get the last score

problems.title: Changing an item
problems.content: You can change any item in a list by assigning a new value to its index — just like changing a variable!
problems.task: Run this code. The teacher re-marked your second test — your score should be 100, not 92! Watch how the list updates.
problems.hint: Notice how 92 at index 1 gets replaced with 100.

problems.title: Your turn: change an item
problems.content: Now you try! Change the last fruit in the list.
problems.task: Add a line between the list and the print to change 'cherry' (at index 2) to 'mango'. The output should be ['apple', 'banana', 'mango'].
problems.hint: Add fruits[2] = 'mango' between the first line and the print.

rules.message: Use fruits[2] to change the last item

problems.title: How many items?
problems.content: The len() function tells you how many items are in a list. It stands for "length".
problems.task: Run this code to count the animals.
problems.hint: len() returns the total number of items — here it's 4.

problems.title: Print a list and its length
problems.content: You can give print() multiple things separated by commas and it prints them all with spaces in between. This is an easy way to show a list alongside its length!
problems.task: Run this code to see the list and its length printed together.
problems.hint: print() puts a space between each comma-separated item automatically.

problems.title: Your own list from scratch
problems.content: You've been editing existing lists — now create one entirely from scratch! This time, make a list of numbers.
problems.task: Create a list called scores containing exactly these five numbers: 90, 75, 88, 62, 95. Then print the list and its length, like: print('Scores:', scores, 'Count:', len(scores)).
problems.hint: Start with scores = [90, 75, 88, 62, 95]. Then: print('Scores:', scores, 'Count:', len(scores)).

problems.title: Two ways to loop through a list
problems.content:
You already know how to loop through a list to get each item:

for animal in animals:
  print(animal)        # prints: cat, dog, fish

But sometimes you need the position number (index) too. Use range(len(my_list)) to loop through the indices:

for i in range(len(animals)):
  print(i, animals[i])  # prints: 0 cat, 1 dog, 2 fish

The first way is simpler — use it when you just need the items. Use the second way when you need to know where each item is in the list.
problems.footer: for item in list → gives items. for i in range(len(list)) → gives indices.

problems.title: Loop through items
problems.content: You already know this from Worksheet 3! You can loop through every item in a list.
problems.task: Run this code to print each animal on its own line.
problems.hint: The loop picks each item from the list, one at a time.

problems.title: Loop with index numbers
problems.content: This is the new way! range(len(temps)) gives you the numbers 0, 1, 2 — the valid indices. Then you use temps[i] to get each item.
problems.task: Run this code to see each temperature printed with its index number.
problems.hint: range(len(temps)) gives 0, 1, 2 — the valid index numbers.

problems.title: Make a numbered list
problems.content: Let's combine index looping with f-strings to make a nice numbered list! Since Python indices start at 0, we add 1 to make human-friendly numbers.
problems.task: The list currently numbers items starting from 0. Change {i} to {i + 1} so the numbering starts at 1 instead.
problems.hint: Change {i} to {i + 1} inside the f-string.

rules.message: Add 1 to i so numbering starts at 1

problems.title: Your own numbered list
problems.content: Now write a numbered list from scratch!
problems.task: Create a list of your 4 favourite games (or books, movies — anything you like!). Print each one with a number, like: 1. Minecraft.
problems.hint: Create a list, then use for i in range(len(my_list)): and print(f"{i + 1}. {my_list[i]}").

rules.message: Use range(len(...)) to loop with indices

problems.title: Growing a list with append
problems.content: You can add new items to the end of a list using .append(). The list gets bigger!
problems.task: Run this code to see the list grow from 2 items to 3.
problems.hint: Notice how the list starts with 2 items and grows to 3 after .append().

problems.title: Append your own items
problems.content: Now add more items to the list yourself!
problems.task: Add 'mango' and 'grape' to the list using two more .append() lines. The final list should have 5 items.
problems.hint: Add fruits.append('mango') and fruits.append('grape') before the print.

rules.message: Use .append() to add items to the list

problems.title: The append trap!
problems.content: A very common mistake! .append() changes the list directly and returns None. If you save the result, you lose your list!
problems.task: Run this code to see the bug — it prints None! Fix it by removing numbers = from the line with .append().
problems.hint: The line should just be numbers.append(4) — don't try to save the result.

problems.title: Build a list in a loop
problems.content: One of the most useful patterns: start with an empty list [] and use .append() inside a loop to build it up!
problems.task: Write code that creates an empty list, then uses a loop to append the numbers 1 to 5 into it, and prints the final list. The output should be [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].
problems.hint: Start with numbers = []. Then for i in range(1, 6): and numbers.append(i) inside the loop. Print after the loop.

rules.message: Use .append() to add items to the list
Use a for loop to build the list

rules.solutionCode:
numbers = []
for i in range(1, 6):
  numbers.append(i)
print(numbers)

problems.title: Filtering with if
problems.content: Lists and if statements are a powerful combo! You can loop through a list and only act on items that match a condition.
problems.task: This code prints all the numbers. Add an if statement inside the loop so it only prints numbers greater than 5.
problems.hint: Add if num > 5: before the print, and indent the print under the if statement.

rules.message: Use an if statement to filter the numbers

problems.title: Shopping list with f-strings
problems.content: Let's put several skills together! Use a list, a loop with indices, and f-strings to make a nicely formatted shopping list.
problems.task: Write a program that creates a list of at least 4 shopping items, then prints each one numbered like: 1. Bread, 2. Milk, etc.
problems.hint: Create your list, then use for i in range(len(items)): with print(f"{i + 1}. {items[i]}").

rules.message: Use range(len(...)) to loop with indices
Use an f-string to format the output

problems.title: Square numbers
problems.content: Let's combine input, loops, and append to build a list from scratch!
problems.task: Get a number n from the user, then build a list of the first n square numbers (1, 4, 9, 16, ...) using a loop and .append(). Print the final list.
problems.hint: Start with squares = []. Loop with for i in range(1, n + 1): and append i * i. Print the list after the loop.

rules.message: Use .append() to add items to the list
Use a for loop

rules.solutionCode:
n = get_input()
squares = []
for i in range(1, n + 1):
  squares.append(i * i)
print(squares)

problems.title: Challenge: Find the biggest
problems.content: Finding the largest number in a list is a classic programming task. The trick is to keep track of the biggest number you've seen so far as you go through the list.
problems.task: Write a program that finds and prints the biggest number in the list. Use a variable to remember the biggest so far, and update it inside the loop.
problems.hint: Start with biggest = numbers[0]. Loop through the list with for num in numbers:. If num > biggest, update biggest. Print it after the loop.

rules.message: Use the numbers list in your solution
Loop through the numbers list
Use an if statement to check each number

problems.title: Challenge: Count the matches
problems.content: Can you count how many times a specific number appears in a list? This uses the accumulator pattern from Worksheet 3 — but instead of adding numbers, you're counting!
problems.task: Get a target number from the user and count how many times it appears in the list. Print the count.
problems.hint: Start with count = 0. Loop through numbers. If the number equals the target, add 1 to count. Print count after the loop.

rules.message: Use a for loop
Use an if statement to check each number

rules.solutionCode:
numbers = [1, 3, 5, 3, 7, 3, 9, 1]
target = get_input()
count = 0
for num in numbers:
  if num == target:
    count = count + 1
print(count)

problems.title: Creative challenge: Your list program
problems.content: Time to be creative! Use what you've learned about lists to build something cool.
problems.task: Write a program that creates a list and does something interesting with it. You must use a list, a for loop, and at least one of: indexing, .append(), len(), or an if statement. Show your teacher!
problems.hint: Some ideas: a quiz with answers stored in a list, a top-5 countdown, a program that collects even numbers into a new list, or a favourite-things ranker. Anything goes!

rules.message: Your program should use a list
Your program should use a for loop
